<Summary source="https://homeland.house.gov/2026/02/24/guest-in-the-washington-reporter-the-state-of-the-border-showcases-a-year-of-winning-even-as-democrats-hold-dhs-funding-hostage/">
## Summary: "The State of the Border Showcases a Year of Winning" (Homeland.house, February 24, 2026)

**Source context:** This is an op-ed by Rep. Michael Guest (R-MS), Chairman of the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement, published in the Washington Reporter and republished via the House Committee on Homeland Security website. It is an explicitly partisan document promoting Trump administration border policies.

---

### Key Statistics Cited (attributed to CBP data):

- **January 2026:** CBP recorded **9,726 Southwest border encounters** — described as the third-lowest month on recent record, and **94% below January 2024**
- **February 2025–January 2026:** Nationwide border encounters dropped **83%** compared to the same period under Biden (when CBP recorded ~2.1 million encounters)
- **January 2026 nationwide encounters:** Down **85%** compared to January 2024
- **June 2025:** Monthly Southwest border encounters fell **below 10,000** for the first time in years
- **Post-inauguration (first 11 days):** Encounters at Southwest border ports of entry dropped **93%** vs. the last 11 days of Biden's term
- **Daily known gotaways** dropped to ~132/day by early February 2025, down from ~1,800/day in 2023
- **January 2026:** Narcotic seizures increased **15%** compared to December 2025
- **Ninth consecutive month** of zero parole releases of inadmissible aliens (as of January 2026)

### Policy/Operational Context Relevant to Future Months:
- DHS was facing a **second government shutdown** at time of writing (February 24, 2026), which the author warns could disrupt border security progress
- The "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" provided reconciliation funding described as covering short-term CBP needs
- A **full-year DHS funding bill** passed the House but was being blocked by Senate Democrats as of publication date
- Planned investments include: 700 miles of border wall, 3,000 new Border Patrol agents, 5,000 new customs officers, 200 new Air and Marine Operations agents

---

*Note: This article does not contain data for February or March 2026. The most recent data point provided is January 2026 (9,726 Southwest border encounters). The article is politically framed and should be interpreted accordingly, though the CBP statistics cited appear to reference official agency data.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/border/2024/02/14/477546/migration-along-the-u-s-mexico-border-dropped-significantly-in-january/">
## Summary: U.S. Southwest Border Encounters Drop Significantly in January 2024

**Source:** Houston Public Media / Texas Public Radio | **Date:** February 14, 2024 | **Authors:** Marian Navarro & Dan Katz

### Key Statistics
- **U.S. Border Patrol encounters between ports of entry (January 2024):** 124,220
- **Total encounters (including ports of entry and CBP One appointments):** 176,205
- **Month-over-month change:** Down **42% from December 2023**
- **Between ports of entry change:** Down **50% from December 2023**
- This was the **lowest recorded total** in Fiscal Year 2024 at the time of reporting
- Despite the drop, January 2024 encounters were **still higher** than the same month in prior years

### Official Statements
- **Troy Miller (Acting CBP Commissioner):** Attributed the decline to seasonal trends and enhanced enforcement by CBP and international partners
- **Miller** also acknowledged "serious challenges along our border which surpass the capacity of the immigration system"
- **Mia Ehrenberg (DHS spokesperson):** Criticized the House impeachment of Secretary Mayorkas as lacking constitutional grounds

### Contextual Factors Noted
- U.S. and Mexican officials had been meeting regularly following record December arrivals
- Mexico increased patrols and cracked down on smuggling networks under U.S. pressure
- A migrant encampment of ~1,500 people in Matamoros was cleared by Mexican officials
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://www.scotusblog.com/2025/09/what-the-trump-administration-wants-from-the-supreme-court-this-term/">
## Summary

**Disclaimer:** This article is entirely about Trump administration legal strategies at the Supreme Court level (birthright citizenship, Second Amendment rights for drug users, asylum seeker policies, and tariffs). It contains **no information relevant** to U.S. Customs and Border Protection Southwest Land Border Encounter statistics or migration numbers.

---

### Key Points from the Article (for completeness):

- The Trump administration is preparing a cert petition to bring the **birthright citizenship executive order** before the Supreme Court in the 2025-26 term.
- The administration filed cert petitions in **five cases** asking the Supreme Court to rule on whether federal law prohibiting habitual drug users from possessing firearms violates the Second Amendment; it specifically wants the Court to take *United States v. Hemani* (5th Circuit).
- In **Noem v. Al Otro Lado**, the administration is asking the Supreme Court to define when an asylum seeker "arrives in the United States" under the Immigration and Nationality Act — arguing physical border crossing is the threshold, not presenting oneself to an official (even in Mexico). The 9th Circuit ruled against the administration.
- The administration also filed a cert petition challenging a Federal Circuit ruling against Trump's **tariffs**.

None of these legal proceedings contain data or projections related to Southwest Land Border Encounter statistics.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.latimes.com/world-nation/story/2025-03-04/as-trump-shuts-the-door-on-asylum-seekers-migrants-turn-to-mexico">
## Article Summary: Asylum Requests Surge in Mexico Amid U.S. Border Crackdown

**Source:** Appears to be a major U.S. newspaper (likely Los Angeles Times or Washington Post, based on context clues), date not specified but references events in early 2025.

---

### Key Facts & Statistics

- Mexico's annual asylum applications grew **from 1,295 in 2013 to a record 140,982 in 2023** — roughly a hundredfold increase over a decade.
- An official familiar with Mexican government figures stated asylum applications are now **three to four times greater than pre-November 2024 levels**, with **as many as 1,000 migrants per day** beginning the process.
- Approximately **270,000 people** had been waiting in Mexico using the Biden-era CBP One app when Trump cancelled it on his first day in office.
- One migrant shelter in Villahermosa saw asylum applications jump from **106 in December to 224 in January**.
- Roughly **$2 billion in annual U.S. aid** to Latin America and the Caribbean is currently frozen, forcing nonprofits to lay off staff or suspend operations.

### Named Source Opinions

- **Gretchen Kuhner** (Director, Institute for Women in Migration): Called the situation "worse than anything I've ever seen," citing both the policy shift and sudden aid withdrawal.
- **Andrés Ramírez** (former director, Mexican Commission for Refugee Assistance): Said Mexico's economy can absorb migrants but that strengthening institutional capacity will be required.
- **Josue Leal** (shelter director, Oasis De Paz del Espíritu Santo Amparito): Described Mexico as doing "the dirty work for the United States"; lost half his staff due to the aid freeze.

### Contextual Relevance to Border Encounters Question

- The article strongly implies **U.S. Southwest Land Border encounters have dropped sharply**, describing the border as "the quietest it's been in years" due to Trump administration policies stopping asylum processing there.
- The reduction in U.S. border encounters appears to be a direct consequence of migrants being redirected or stranded in Mexico rather than reaching the U.S. border.

---

*Disclaimer: The article appears to be cut off near the end, so some concluding information may be missing.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://journalistsresource.org/home/covering-immigration-stories-in-2025-3-reporting-tips-and-a-linked-list-of-data-resources/">
## Summary: "Covering Immigration in 2025: Reporting Tips and Data Resources" (*The Journalist's Resource*, March 11, 2025)

This article recaps a webinar hosted by The Journalist's Resource on data-driven immigration journalism. It offers reporting tips and a curated list of data sources relevant to immigration coverage.

### Key Reporting Tips

1. **Avoid superlative/alarmist framing**: Panelist Caitlin Dickerson cautioned against "record numbers" headlines, urging journalists to consult longer historical data (30–50 years) rather than just recent years. Historical graphs show comparable border encounter spikes in the early 1980s and early 2000s.

2. **Validate government data carefully**: Panelist Kocher warned that ICE detention data — published roughly twice monthly per congressional mandate — frequently contains errors, advising journalists to vet figures before citing them.

3. **Clarify legal migration pathways**: Panelist Dakin-Grimm noted that U.S. immigration law offers only four paths to legal permanent residency, and that most asylum seekers at the southern border will not meet the legal threshold for asylum qualification.

### Key Data Sources Highlighted (Relevant to the Forecasting Question)
- **CBP Public Data Portal**: Publishes migrant encounter datasets and dashboards, updated monthly since FY2020.
- **Adam Isacson's searchable migration data tool** (WOLA): Simplifies CBP's large encounter datasets into a searchable format.
- **TRAC**: Provides FOIA-derived immigration enforcement data not always available on government sites.

**Note**: This article does not contain specific numerical data on Southwest Land Border Encounters and is primarily a journalism resource guide.
</Summary>

**Mexico Confirms No Mexican Citizens Affected by Middle East Conflict as of March 2, 2026**
On March 2, 2026, the Mexican Secretariat of Foreign Relations (SRE) confirmed that as of Monday afternoon, there are no registered cases of Mexicans affected physically by the conflict in the Middle East following attacks in Iran. The SRE stated in a press release: 'No hay personas mexicanas afectadas en su integridad física por los hechos registrados, hasta este momento' (There are no Mexican people affected in their physical integrity by the recorded events, up to this moment). The SRE maintains permanent attention on the situation, and Mexican embassies continue to contact nationals in the region, both residents and those in transit. Mexican representations have deployed protection and consular assistance protocols, assisting some individuals in exiting via safe land routes from Israel and Jordan. However, the SRE noted that 'gran parte del espacio aéreo permanece cerrado y los vuelos que se han restablecido son aún muy limitados' (a large part of the airspace remains closed and the restored flights are still very limited), advising against travel to the region. Travelers in transit are urged to contact airlines, stay in safe places, and reach out to embassies via provided phone numbers.

**Mexico's Diplomatic Pivot: Diversifying Alliances Amidst US Tariff Threats and T-MEC Review**
According to internationalist and UNAM professor José Joel Peña Llanes, writing for El Heraldo de México on March 03, 2026, Mexico faces a critical geopolitical challenge under the second term of Donald Trump, which began in January 2025. The US administration has signaled that the scheduled review of the T-MEC in July 2026 will be stringent, with tariffs returning to the center of negotiations. For three decades, Mexico's economy has expanded into North America, creating a dependency on integration with the United States and, to a lesser extent, Canada, under the NAFTA and T-MEC frameworks. This integration has driven export growth but failed to automatically translate into commercial diversification due to geographic constraints, regional value chains, and logistics. To mitigate risks associated with President Trump's threats, Mexico has adjusted its trade policy, including a tariff increase on products from countries without a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) effective January 1, 2026. This measure specifically impacts imports from Asian countries, including China, which is identified as the second-largest source of imports. While the stated goal is industrial protection, the move sends a geopolitical message to avoid being perceived as a 'bridge' for Chinese goods to the US market, though Mexico seeks to avoid opening a new front with its northern neighbor. Beijing responded with an investigation into potential trade barriers, arguing that the T-MEC review should not condition Mexico's relationship with third parties. The article posits that the solution lies in diversification and building options beyond mere sales. Potential new alliances include energy, financing, and innovation cooperation with the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC), and investment and connectivity opportunities with the African Union and the Continental Free Trade Area. The European Union remains a strategic partner, with an interim commercial agreement expected to be signed while the modernized Global Agreement advances ratification. Peña Llanes argues that the fundamental shift required is political: Mexico must move from an ideologized foreign policy to a pragmatic one that prioritizes results and risk management over internal political affinities. The author warns that allowing foreign policy to become an extension of the internal political project reduces negotiation margins and limits the construction of stable, useful alliances. In an environment of growing pressure and uncertainty, expanding the agenda and adding allies is described as a necessity for maneuverability.
Original language: es
Publish date: March 03, 2026 12:38 AM
Source:[El Heraldo de M�xico](https://heraldodemexico.com.mx/opinion/2026/3/3/mexico-mas-alla-de-norteamerica-abrir-la-agenda-sumar-aliados-775116.html)

**U.S. Trade Agenda Highlights USMCA Concerns Over Mexico's Energy Regulations and Canada's Dairy Policies**
On March 2, 2026, the U.S. Department of Commerce released its '2026 Trade Policy Agenda', identifying 'many problems' requiring resolution during the review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA/T-MEC), according to the article published by El Universal. The U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) claimed that Mexico has adopted preferential measures benefiting national champions in the energy and mining sectors, specifically regarding oil, gas, and electricity, to the detriment of U.S. investors. The USTR further alleged that Mexico has undermined its investment climate through inadequate labor laws and lax regulatory protection, which continues to harm U.S. workers. Additionally, the USTR asserted that Canada maintains policies violating the USMCA in the dairy sector, restricting U.S. product entry, and enforces discriminatory measures and restrictions in the digital sector, such as decrees regulating online broadcasts. With ministers from the three countries set to convene in a few months to review compliance, the U.S. Commerce Department emphasized the need to strengthen 'rules of origin' and combat transshipment and offshoring. The agency highlighted the region's critical importance to U.S. national and economic security, particularly Mexico and Canada. Urgent challenges identified include increased investment by non-market economy domiciled companies and the impact of overcapacity, such as steel production, on all three partner nations. The agenda outlined six central points: continuing the Reciprocal Trade Agreements (ART) program; enforcing U.S. trade agreements and laws; strengthening supply chains in critical minerals and strategic sectors; trading with China based on reciprocity and balance; and promoting U.S. interests in multilateral forums. The USTR stated that due to aggressive market liberalization, the U.S. market is 'more open to foreign goods and services than the markets of its trading partners are to U.S. exports,' prompting a push to reverse the trade deficit through the 'America First' policy to boost domestic production.

Publish date: March 02, 2026 09:03 PM
Source:[El Universal](https://www.eluniversal.com.mx/cartera/eu-advierte-que-hay-muchos-problemas-en-el-t-mec-senala-regulaciones-de-mexico-en-energia-y-mineria/)

**Mexico Remittances Drop 1.4% in January 2026 Amid U.S. Policy Shifts**
According to official data from the Banco de México (Banxico) cited by La Jornada, remittances sent from the United States to Mexico decreased in January 2026, with inflows totaling $4,594 million, a 1.4% annual decline compared to the $4,660 million recorded in January 2025. This figure confirms a weakening trend that began in 2025, which marked the first annual drop in remittances in over a decade, registering a 4.56% decrease for the full year. The decline is attributed to changes in U.S. immigration policy under the second term of Donald Trump and new conditions for international money transfers. In addition to the reduction in total funds, the number of transactions fell by 5.2%, dropping from 12,084,000 transfers in January 2025 to 11,461,000 in January 2026. This decrease in transaction volume is interpreted as a signal that fewer individuals are sending money or doing so less frequently. Cumulative data from February 2025 to January 2026 shows a 4.8% annual decrease compared to the same period in the previous year. Despite these reductions, digital services and transfer platforms remain the primary channel for sending funds. Banxico reports that these remittances continue to be a critical source of foreign exchange for Mexico, directly impacting the ability of millions of families to cover basic expenses such as food, housing, education, and health.

**Mexico Remittances Fall 1.4% in Jan 2026 Amid US Tax Policy**
In January 2026, Mexico received US$4,594 million in remittances, a decrease of 1.4% compared to US$4,660 million in the same month of 2025, according to the Bank of Mexico (Banxico). The average individual remittance was US$401, an increase of 3.9% over the US$385 recorded in January 2025. The remittance account surplus was US$4,495 million, down from US$4,551 million in January 2025. Conversely, residents in Mexico sent US$99 million abroad, representing a year-over-year contraction of 9.3%. For the full year of 2025, Mexico received US$61,791 million, marking a 4.6% annual decline and the first yearly drop after 11 consecutive years of growth, as reported by the World Bank. This downturn is attributed to the aggressive anti-immigration policies of the United States. In June 2026, the U.S. government announced a 1% tax on cash remittances, money orders, and similar instruments. In response, Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum implemented a special program to reimburse nationals for the costs associated with sending dollars to Mexico and criticized the U.S. measure as a violation of the 1994 bilateral double taxation treaty. These figures were released following the announcement of a 0.6% growth in Mexico's GDP for 2025 and a 1.8% increase from October to December, allowing the country to avoid recession after two consecutive quarters of decline.

**China's National Immigration Administration Reports Record Visa-Free Arrivals and Successful Crackdown on Border Crimes Following 2025 Two Sessions**
On March 2, 2026, a spokesperson for the National Immigration Administration of China announced that the agency successfully processed 194 suggestions and proposals from deputies and members of the 2025 National Two Sessions (93 from NPC deputies and 101 from CPPCC members), with all cases completed on time and with high quality. To address societal demands for improved immigration management, the Administration implemented 10 innovative measures, including adding Indonesia to the 240-hour visa-free transit list, designating five ports in Guangdong for this policy, expanding visa-free and mutual visa waiver agreements, and promoting smart clearance at ports connecting with Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan. These measures contributed to record-breaking figures in 2025: a total of 697 million inbound and outbound personnel inspections, and 30.08 million visa-free foreign arrivals, representing a 49.5% year-on-year increase. In terms of law enforcement, the Administration investigated over 18,900 cases of crimes endangering national border management, apprehended more than 54,600 suspects, solved 438 drug-related cases, seized 6.09 tons of drugs, 2,098 firearms, and 64,500 rounds of ammunition. The spokesperson stated that with the 2026 National Two Sessions approaching, the Administration will continue to actively adopt suggestions to convert them into practical actions serving high-quality development and maintaining border security.

**China's Immigration Administration Reports Record Cross-Border Traffic and Crackdown Success in 2025**
On March 2, 2026, the National Immigration Administration of China announced that it successfully completed 194 suggestions and proposals from deputies and members of the National Two Sessions in 2025, including 93 suggestions from deputies to the National People's Congress and 101 proposals from members of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference. According to the administration's spokesperson, all proposals were completed on time with high quality. In response to recommendations regarding regional development, visa-free policies, cross-border facilitation for Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan, and smart port construction, the administration introduced 10 innovative measures. These include adding Indonesia to the 240-hour visa-free transit policy, designating five ports in Guangdong for the policy, expanding the scope of unilateral and mutual visa-free agreements, and promoting smart clearance at ports connected to Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan. As a result, in 2025, immigration agencies across China processed 697 million entries and exits, setting a new record, while visa-free arrivals reached 30.08 million foreigners, a 49.5% year-on-year increase. Regarding border security and crime suppression, the administration launched the 'Menglie-2025' special operation to combat crimes related to border management, governance of 'three nones' (illegal entry, stay, and work) foreigners, and drug trafficking. In 2025, agencies investigated over 18,900 cases of border management crimes, arrested over 54,600 suspects, solved 438 drug cases, seized 6.09 tons of drugs, 2,098 guns, and 64,500 rounds of ammunition. The spokesperson stated that with the 2026 Two Sessions approaching, the administration will continue to actively adopt suggestions and improve the quality of its work to address public concerns.

**Sheinbaum's New Intelligence-Led Security Strategy Yields Major Cartel Takedowns**
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo, acting as Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces, has executed a new security strategy centered on 'intelligence and research' to combat organized crime, resulting in what the article describes as positive outcomes. Key events include the death of Nemesio Rubén Oseguera Cervantes, alias 'El Mencho', leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), during a federal operation in Tapalpa, Jalisco; the arrest of Diego Rivera, the mayor of Tequila, who was accused of extortion and corruption; and the detention and trial of Hernán Bermúdez Requena, former security secretary of Tabasco (2019-2021) and leader of the 'La Barredora' criminal group. According to the text, 35,817 people have been detained for 'high-impact crimes', with firearms seized and drugs confiscated along the US-Mexico border. The article outlines a three-part security plan: 1) intense combat against organized crime and corruption networks, 2) recovery of social peace on streets and highways, and 3) territorial order with a strong state presence. The author criticizes national media coverage of the El Mencho operation for failing to contextualize the event within this broader federal security policy, noting that headlines on February 23, 2026 (from outlets like Milenio, El Financiero, and Reforma) focused on details rather than the strategic context. The strategy is framed as a response to intensified pressure from the US government and the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup, which Mexico hosts in Guadalajara, Monterrey, and Mexico City. The article claims Mexico holds the third position in 'government recognition' among OECD members, behind Norway and Sweden. Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch is quoted stating, 'There is crime there and it will be pursued,' while President Sheinbaum emphasized that 'all operations are carried out by federal (Mexican) forces. There is no participation of United States forces. What there is is a lot of information exchange.' The piece concludes by calling for a 'pedagogical labor' from journalism to help citizens discern truth from disinformation generated by criminal organizations and bots ahead of the 2027 elections.

**Mexico Sees Sharp Drop in U.S. Border Weapon Seizures Despite Appeals for Help**
Despite the Mexican Government's repeated calls for greater support from the United States to curb the flow of weapons, statistics from the U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) indicate a marked decline in seizures north of the border in 2025. According to CBP data, 1,171 devices heading toward Mexican territory were detected in fiscal year 2023, dropping to 1,110 in 2024 and 606 in 2025. In the current fiscal year 2026 (which began in October 2025), official information shows that 743 weapons have been seized as of January. The lowest quantities were found during inspections of individuals leaving San Diego, California, where U.S. officers stopped the entry of 4 weapons in 2023, 10 in 2024, 20 in 2025, and only 3 in the current period. The second port with the lowest figures is El Paso, Texas, with 26, 22, 23, and 12 units stopped in the respective fiscal years. In contrast, Laredo recorded 982, 910, and 473 weapons in 2023, 2024, and 2025, with 718 seized so far in 2026, while Tucson, Arizona, reported 159, 168, 90, and 10. On September 7, 2022, former Mexican Secretary of Foreign Affairs Marcelo Ebrard, while on a tour in Ciudad Juárez, stated he expected U.S. government assistance to reduce this traffic, saying, 'We want the U.S. Government to help us also to reduce that traffic coming to Mexico. We stop them on this side, on the Mexican side, so help them on the border so they don't pass.' On February 26, 2026, during a morning press conference in the context of the elimination of the alleged leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, Nemesio Oseguera, President Claudia Sheinbaum reiterated that 'one of the central points' of the relationship with the neighboring country is the reduction of these inflows, stating, 'Do they want to help us? They can help us in many ways, one of them: stop the entry of weapons from the United States to Mexico.'

**Mexico and US Joint Operation Eliminates CJNG Leader El Mencho, Signaling End of Impunity**
The capture and killing of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as 'El Mencho', leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), places the Mexican government at a critical turning point in national security history, according to an article published by Excélsior on March 1, 2026. The operation, a tactical blow to the CJNG's heart, was executed by the government of President Claudia Sheinbaum in coordination with the United States, signaling the end of impunity for drug lords. Sara Carter, Director of the White House Office of National Drug Control Policy, stated during meetings in Mexico on February 27 and 28, 2026, that 'the death of El Mencho sends a message to all sicarios and all cartel bosses... if the Mexican government, working with the US, could catch El Mencho, we can go after you and we will do it, there will be no place to hide, no place you can go, because we will find you, we will catch you and your time will be over' (Excélsior, 27/02/2026). While the Mexican government faced violent resistance including vehicle burnings, road blockades, and attacks on civil infrastructure, the Navy, Army, and National Guard maintained their position without retreat. The article emphasizes that eliminating El Mencho is merely the prologue; the government must now focus on financial disarticulation and immediate occupation of operational bases to prevent successor factions from launching a bloody offensive. The strategy requires permanent state presence in states like Michoacán, Jalisco, and Zacatecas, rather than temporary security measures for the World Cup, and involves educating children to reject the 'grandiosity' of cartel life.

**Nicaragua Drops Bombshell Decision, Shutting Down Key Migration Route for Cubans Headed to the US - What This Means for Thousands of Desperate Travelers! - Travel And Tour World**
Nicaragua has ended its visa-free entry policy for Cuban citizens, effective Sunday, February 9, 2026, marking a significant shift in regional migration dynamics. The policy change, which had been in place since 2021, allowed thousands of Cubans to transit through Nicaragua—flying into Managua and then journeying overland through Honduras, Guatemala, and Mexico to reach the U.S. southern border. This route had become a key corridor for Cuban migrants fleeing economic collapse, political repression, and shortages in Cuba. The decision is widely believed to be a response to sustained diplomatic pressure from the U.S. government, which had criticized Nicaragua’s policy for contributing to rising illegal crossings at the U.S.-Mexico border. The Biden administration has imposed sanctions on Nicaragua under President Daniel Ortega, and this policy shift may be an effort to ease tensions. Experts warn that closing this route will likely force Cubans to seek more dangerous, irregular pathways, increasing reliance on human smugglers and raising risks of trafficking, exploitation, and violence. The move may also create regional instability, as neighboring countries face increased migration pressure. While the U.S. may view this as a step toward curbing illegal immigration, the broader humanitarian consequences are significant, as political decisions in Nicaragua now directly impact the lives of thousands of vulnerable migrants. The long-term effects on migration patterns across Central America remain uncertain.
Original language: en
Publish date: February 09, 2026 01:11 AM
Source:[Travel And Tour World](https://www.travelandtourworld.com/news/article/nicaragua-drops-bombshell-decision-shutting-down-key-migration-route-for-cubans-headed-to-the-us-what-this-means-for-thousands-of-desperate-travelers/)

**Number of Migrants Detained in Mexico for U.S. Transit Plummets Amid U.S. Border Crackdown**
According to data from Mexico's National Institute of Migration (INM), detentions of irregular migrants in Mexico dropped by 92.2% between February and November 2025 compared to the same period in 2024, coinciding with the hardening of U.S. immigration policy under President Donald Trump’s first year in office. In 2024, over one million migrant apprehensions were recorded attempting to cross from Mexico to the U.S., but in 2025, the number fell to just over 81,000. The shift was particularly sharp after the U.S. presidential transition in January 2025: over 64,000 detentions occurred in the final days of Joe Biden’s presidency, but within one month of Trump’s inauguration, the number dropped to 24,000. Deportations (official returns) also declined significantly, with fewer than 10,000 assisted returns carried out by Mexico from February to November 2025—less than half the number from the previous year. For migrants not deported immediately, the INM implemented alternatives: 84% received a notice allowing them to leave shelters and depart Mexico voluntarily within 30 days, though the compliance rate is unspecified. 'The data confirms that the Republican president’s border closure policies and deterrent announcements have achieved their intended effect.'

**From Ally to Problem: How Project 2025 Redefines Mexico in U.S. Strategy**
The article examines how Mexico has shifted from being perceived as a strategic ally to being framed as a central problem in U.S. policy under the influence of Project 2025, a comprehensive conservative political blueprint developed by over 50 right-wing organizations. Spearheaded by Paul Dans and Steven Groves—former Trump administration officials and legal experts—the plan is structured around the principle 'personnel is policy,' asserting that control over federal appointments determines policy direction. The project outlines detailed, agency-by-agency reforms targeting institutions including the White House, DHS, DOJ, State Department, Intelligence Community, Treasury, and Commerce. It redefines national security by framing migration, organized crime, drug trafficking, and border issues as existential threats to U.S. sovereignty, elevating them to global risk status. Under this framework, Mexico is repositioned not as a partner but as a territory of transit, origin, and risk—justifying coercive measures such as designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations (FTO/SDTO), imposing financial sanctions, and expanding the definition of material support. The strategy prioritizes enforcement, expulsion, and deterrence over cooperation or development. This structural shift, driven by a vision of a highly centralized, action-oriented executive, reduces diplomatic space, normalizes selective cooperation, and anticipates recurring diplomatic tensions. The ultimate goal of Project 2025 is not merely to address Mexico or drug trafficking, but to empower the U.S. executive branch to operate without internal or external constraints, transforming the Western Hemisphere into a perimeter of security and the border into a line of defense. As a result, Mexico risks becoming strategically indispensable as a problem rather than a partner—due to proximity, not malice. The article notes that during a January 14, 2026, press conference, the Mexican president confirmed that a military action was ruled out. The analysis is grounded in the 900-page Project 2025 document, which reflects a structural, legal, and institutional reorientation of U.S. foreign and domestic policy.

**The Border Crackdown Poised To Be Pivotal For Republicans In 2026 Senate Races**
Senate Majority Leader John Thune, along with Republican leaders including Sen. John Cornyn, North Carolina Senate candidate Michael Whatley, and Michigan candidate Mike Rogers, visited the U.S.-Mexico border wall in McAllen, Texas, on January 9, 2026, to highlight border security as a central theme for the GOP’s 2026 Senate campaign strategy. Thune stated on X that 'thanks to new leadership in Washington and strong policies like the Working Families Tax Cuts, the southern border is safe and secure.' The visit underscored the growing political emphasis on immigration, a top voter concern in 2024 and expected to remain pivotal in 2026. Cornyn, facing a tight primary challenge from Attorney General Ken Paxton and Rep. Wesley Hunt, defended his border record, citing over $13 billion in federal reimbursements for state border efforts and multiple votes for border wall funding. A social media dispute erupted between Cornyn’s campaign and Hunt, who questioned Cornyn’s commitment to border security. Meanwhile, apprehensions at the southern border dropped to just 221 on January 8, 2026, according to Fox News, a dramatic decline from the Biden-era peak of thousands per day. This decline follows major immigration legislation signed by President Trump in 2025, including the Laken Riley Act and the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' (OBBA), which allocated billions in border security funding and $13.5 billion in reimbursements to states. The Cook Political Report rates both Whatley’s and Rogers’s races as 'toss-ups,' with the eventual Republican nominee set to face either Rep. Jasmine Crockett or state Rep. James Talarico in a competitive border-state contest.

**Hope in the EU Amid Escalating Migrant Crackdown in the U.S.**
A tamales vendor's abandoned cart in Los Angeles, California—left behind after the Mexican migrant owner was detained and deported in late 2025—symbolizes a growing humanitarian crisis in the United States, described by activists and lawyers as an 'unprecedented migrant hunt' and a policy of terror. Since January 2025, when Donald Trump began his second presidential term, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has deported 605,000 immigrants. Abogada Raquel M. Kuronen stated that these raids are unprecedented in scale and brutality, with ICE conducting operations at schools, churches, Home Depots, auto washes, and homes—often without warrants—targeting people based on skin color or Spanish-speaking appearance. She reported that citizens and legal residents have been arrested, families separated, and individuals detained for weeks, even after winning court cases. The policy, she said, is 'the worst abuse in the country’s history' and is forcing thousands to self-deport out of fear. In 2026, the situation is expected to worsen with new laws to ban undocumented migrants from opening bank accounts, aiming to economically isolate them. Activist Patty Chávez called 2025 'the worst year for migrants in decades,' noting abandoned food carts, business closures, and increased raids at commercial centers, churches, and schools. She reported that ICE operations are now using coded messages via WhatsApp and social media to warn communities, such as 'the avocados are on that highway' (referring to ICE’s green uniforms). The case of Mexican-American TikToker Richard LA, who was shot by ICE agents in October 2025 after reporting raids, illustrates the violence. DHS also announced that non-citizens will now have biometric data collected at all exits, with those returning to Mexico being detained and deported—making future visa or regularization applications nearly impossible.

**Migrants Opt to Stay in Mexico Amid Trump-Era U.S. Immigration Crackdown, Seeking Stability Over Return**
Since January 20, 2025, the implementation of stricter U.S. immigration policies under Donald Trump’s second presidential term has transformed Mexico from a transit country into a destination for thousands of foreign migrants. Over 11,886 migrants were deported to Mexico between January 20 and December 10, 2025, according to Mexican government data. More than 58,800 foreign nationals applied for asylum with Mexico’s Commission for Assistance to Refugees (Comar) between January and September 2025—nearly identical to the 58,644 applications recorded in the same period in 2024. A survey by ACNUR indicates that 66% of these migrants now consider permanently settling in Mexico, driven by safety concerns that prevent return to their home countries. The number of asylum seekers originates primarily from countries such as Venezuela, Haiti, Honduras, and Guatemala. The situation has worsened due to changes in Comar’s procedures: the discontinuation of physical certification of legal stay and reliance on email notifications, which increases vulnerability to deportations and raids. Additionally, the National Institute of Migration (INM) drastically reduced the issuance of Humanitarian Visitor Cards, which allow work and access to basic services. This has led to overcrowding in shelters, especially in northern border cities like Tijuana, Ciudad Juárez, and Reynosa, where migrants face risks including violence, extreme weather, and inadequate healthcare. Experts, including Luis Javier Carrancá from the Iberoamerican University’s Refugee Legal Clinic and ACNUR’s Giovanni Lepri, emphasize that Mexico is failing to uphold the principle of non-refoulement and lacks sustainable public policies to support integration. Lepri notes that despite over a million job vacancies in Mexico, negative narratives about migrants taking jobs persist. The country now faces a long-term challenge: either establish clear migration rules or continue to leave thousands in legal and humanitarian limbo.

**Trump Plans Major Immigration Raids in 2026 Amid Rising Criticism: Details Revealed**
In 2026, former U.S. President Donald Trump plans to implement a significantly stricter immigration policy, despite growing public opposition ahead of midterm elections. The U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Border Patrol will receive an additional $170 billion by September 2029, far exceeding their current annual budgets of approximately $19 billion. The administration aims to hire thousands of new agents, open new detention centers, increase deportations, transfer immigrants from local jails, and collaborate with private companies to track individuals without legal status. Trump has already deployed immigration agents to major U.S. cities, conducting raids and occasionally engaging in confrontations with residents. While high-profile raids have targeted businesses, they have largely avoided farms, factories, and other employers known for hiring immigrants. Deportations will accelerate, even as political resistance grows. Miami, a city heavily affected by the policy due to its large immigrant population, recently elected its first Democratic mayor in nearly three decades, with the new mayor citing federal actions as a key factor. Political strategist Mike Madrid, a moderate Republican, stated that public perception is shifting from immigration as an issue to one of civil rights violations, due process breaches, and the militarization of neighborhoods in an unconstitutional manner. Trump’s overall approval rating for his immigration policy has dropped from 50% in March (before the crackdowns began) to 41% by mid-December. Public concern is primarily focused on aggressive federal enforcement tactics, including tear gas and forceful arrests. Although Trump previously promised to deport 1 million immigrants annually, he is unlikely to meet this target in 2025. To date, approximately 622,000 immigrants have been deported since Trump’s return to office. Tom Homan, White House Border Director, claims Trump has fulfilled his promise to conduct a historic deportation operation, stop illegal immigration across the U.S.-Mexico border, and remove criminals. Homan confirmed that ICE is expanding staffing and detention capacity due to new funding. A senior administration official stated, 'Next year, you’ll see a sharp increase in arrests,' and confirmed that workplace enforcement checks are 'definitely' part of the plan. Such measures could impact the U.S. economy, as replacing detained immigrant workers may raise labor costs and undermine Trump’s inflation-fighting efforts. Jessica Vaughan, director of policy at the Center for Immigration Studies, a group supportive of cracking down on illegal migration, argued that 'eventually, you’ll have to confront these employers. When that happens, employers will start cleaning up their own acts.' Additionally, as reported earlier by OBOZREVATEL, the U.S. will suspend green card issuance, which grants foreigners indefinite residence and work rights, following a shooting at Brown University.

**U.S. revokes visas of Mexico-based transport executives over migration - UPI.com**
The U.S. State Department revoked the visas of executives and senior officials from a Mexico-based transportation company, citing their alleged role in facilitating illegal migration. According to a State Department spokesman, investigations indicate these individuals arranged transportation for aliens, including minors, from the Caribbean and other regions to transit points in Central America, where many were later intercepted attempting to enter the United States. The move was carried out under a visa-restriction policy announced in March 2024, which empowers the State Department to revoke or deny visas to foreign officials and private-sector actors involved in enabling irregular migration. The policy, an expansion of a Biden-era program, was further extended in 2023 and 2024 to include private transportation providers and now also applies to foreign government officials and state-linked actors. The State Department emphasized that 'the United States will not tolerate any attempts to undermine our national security or immigration laws' and that those profiting from illegal immigration will face consequences. The identities and number of individuals affected were not disclosed. This action is part of a broader immigration crackdown by the Trump administration, which has also paused immigration application processing from 19 countries and deployed National Guard troops to New Orleans. The article was published on December 04, 2025.

**Trump's border crackdown reshapes migration flows   - NaturalNews.com**
The Trump administration's intensified border enforcement policies are reshaping migration flows across Central and South America, according to NaturalNews.com. The Supreme Court recently allowed the termination of Temporary Protected Status (TPS) for over 300,000 Venezuelan migrants, marking a significant victory for executive authority. Officials claim the southern border is now the most secure since 1970, citing steep declines in illegal entries and a deterrent effect that has led migrant caravans—such as a high-profile Cuban-led group—to reroute to destinations like Mexico City. Critics, including Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker, warn that the militarization of border enforcement, including federal military presence in civilian settings, could foreshadow election interference in 2026, raising concerns about ballot security and democratic integrity. Legal pushback continues: federal judges have blocked the transfer of unaccompanied minors into adult detention, halted attempts to end public benefits for undocumented immigrants, and rejected the administration’s use of the 1798 Alien Enemies Act for deporting Venezuelan gang suspects, citing its wartime origins. A second appeals court has again ruled that an executive order to revoke birthright citizenship is unconstitutional. Despite these setbacks, the administration’s broader strategy—framed by supporters as a necessary defense against illegal immigration, human trafficking, and drug cartels—has gained traction. **Illegal Crossings Hit 50-Year Low After Policy Shifts**
Unauthorized crossings at the U.S.-Mexico border have dropped to their lowest levels since the late 1960s or early 1970s, according to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) data, marking a dramatic reversal from the peak of nearly 250,000 apprehensions in December 2023. The decline began in early 2024 following a bipartisan agreement that led Mexico to launch 'decompression' operations, intercepting migrants far from the border and transporting them south. This was reinforced by a June 2024 executive action from the Biden administration that restricted asylum access for most migrants crossing between ports of entry, encouraging lawful processing via the CBP One mobile app. The inauguration of President Donald J. Trump in January 2025 intensified the trend, with a government-wide crackdown enabling swift deportations without asylum hearings, framed as a response to an 'invasion.' Border Patrol Chief Mike Banks stated, 'The greater the punishment, the larger the deterrent,' attributing the success to deterrence policies. Data from transit zones like the Darien Gap also show a sharp slowdown in northward migration. While the reduction is celebrated by border control advocates, critics and humanitarian groups warn it reflects the 'fragile state' of asylum seekers under extreme pressure, with a significant drop in credible fear interviews. Analysts caution that the current low levels are likely temporary without addressing root causes such as political instability, economic distress, and gang violence, and stress that long-term legislative reform is essential for sustainability.

--- Deep Research Analysis ---
Based on my research into current U.S. border policies as of March 2026, I can provide you with comprehensive context regarding the Trump administration's 2026 enforcement crackdown and its specific impact on transit through Mexico.

## The 2026 Enforcement Crackdown Context

The U.S. has implemented an aggressive border enforcement strategy entering 2026, characterized by what the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) reports as nearly 3 million departures of undocumented immigrants over the past 13 months—comprising approximately 2.2 million "self-deportations" and over 713,000 formal deportations [2][9]. This marks the first time since the 1930s that the United States has experienced a net negative migration balance, with more people leaving than entering [5].

## New Policy Measures Affecting Mexican Transit

**Asylum and Work Authorization Restrictions**
A significant new proposal published in the Federal Register would extend the waiting period for asylum seekers to obtain Employment Authorization Documents (EADs) from 180 days to 365 days, requiring migrants to wait at least one year before applying for work permits [10]. Additionally, the administration is reportedly considering freezing all EAD applications for asylum seekers until average processing times drop to 180 days—a threshold that could block work permits for years given that 77% of asylum applications currently take longer than 180 days to resolve [10].

**Maritime Interdictions Off the California Coast**
The crackdown extends beyond land borders. Over the weekend of February 27, 2026, DHS and the Coast Guard interdicted six suspected human smuggling vessels off the San Diego coast, apprehending 82 migrants [2]. This follows a series of maritime operations including the interception of 14 individuals near Point Loma on March 1 and 20 migrants near Sunset Cliffs on February 24, with five vessels carrying 62 migrants intercepted in collaboration with the U.S. Navy [2].

**Third-Country Deportation Restrictions**
A federal court ruling on February 25, 2026, by Boston-based Judge Brian Murphy declared illegal the DHS policy of expelling migrants to third countries, mandating that migrants must be prioritized for return to their country of citizenship before considering deportation to other nations [10]. This directly affects Mexico's role as a transit country, as the ruling requires due process including notification and appeal rights before deportations can proceed [10].

## Transit Route Disruption

**Deterrence at the Guatemala-Mexico Border**
Reports from late February 2026 indicate that enforcement tactics are creating deterrence at transit points before migrants reach the U.S. border. CNN correspondents documented that workers at a transit point on the Guatemala-Mexico border observed a noticeable decline in traffic "in the months after President Trump was inaugurated," with migrants citing fear of being "grabbed by ICE" even if they successfully cross into the United States [20].

**Crackdown on Specific Transit Patterns**
Recent deportations reveal the administration's focus on dismantling complex transit networks through Mexico. On February 28, 2026, the U.S. deported 29 Bangladeshi nationals who had traveled legally to Brazil before entering the U.S. illegally via Mexico—a route that had previously led to rejected asylum applications [18]. This followed the deportation of 36 Bangladeshi nationals on January 20, 2026, highlighting the administration's targeting of extra-continental migrants using Mexico as a transit corridor [18].

## Trade and Transit: The USMCA Dimension

Beyond immigration enforcement, the U.S. has identified "transshipment" concerns in its 2026 Trade Policy Agenda regarding the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA/T-MEC) [3]. While this primarily concerns goods rather than human transit, the Commerce Department has emphasized strengthening "rules of origin" and combating transshipment and offshoring, creating additional friction in U.S.-Mexico border relations [3].

## Operational Challenges and Legal Battles

**Funding Impasse Impacting Enforcement**
As of early March 2026, DHS remains partially shut down due to a funding standoff between the Trump administration and Congressional Democrats over immigration enforcement reforms [8][11]. While ICE and Border Patrol remain funded under the "One Big Beautiful Bill," agencies including the Transportation Security Administration and Coast Guard face funding lapses, potentially affecting the consistency of border operations [8].

**Judicial Pushback**
The enforcement crackdown faces significant judicial headwinds. In addition to the third-country deportation ruling, a federal judge blocked DHS Secretary Kristi Noem's policy requiring seven-day notice for congressional visits to detention facilities, ruling it violated a 2019 appropriations law permitting unannounced oversight [1][4]. These legal challenges create uncertainty about the long-term viability of certain enforcement mechanisms.

## Economic Incentives for "Self-Deportation"

To encourage departure without formal deportation, the administration has raised the incentive for self-deportation to $2,600 plus a free flight to home countries [7]. However, investigations have documented cases where users of the self-deportation app never received promised assistance, and immigration attorneys warn this option does not guarantee future re-entry eligibility [7].

## Key Limitations and Uncertainties

It is important to note that available reporting contains conflicting statistics regarding exact deportation numbers, with sources citing figures ranging from 605,000 to 713,000 formal deportations over similar timeframes [5][7][9]. Additionally, the DHS partial shutdown and ongoing litigation create a fluid situation where enforcement capabilities and legal authorities remain subject to change [11].

The current policy framework represents a multifaceted approach combining restrictive asylum procedures, enhanced maritime and interior enforcement, economic incentives for voluntary departure, and legal pressure on transit routes through Mexico, though its full implementation remains contested in the courts and Congress.
